claim: 2025 US utility-scale generation mix — natural gas 41%, nuclear 18%, coal 17%, wind 11%, solar 7%, hydro 6%, biomass ~1%, geothermal <1%, petroleum 0.7%; fossil fuels ~58%, all renewables 24%; total 4.43 trillion kWh (a record, +2.8% vs 2024); coal is the third-highest source (behind gas and nuclear) and wind+solar combined (18%) edged out coal (17%)

claim: Wind+solar combined (18%) exceeded coal (17%) for a second consecutive year in 2025, and US power-sector emissions rose for a second consecutive year

claim: New transmission capacity does not wait on new generation: it fills in sequence — (1) currently-curtailed existing generation, immediately; (2) queue projects whose binding constraint is connection; (3) new build. Reconductoring is the cork on generation that already exists. Counter-view: curtailment is concentrated in specific regions (West Texas, California) so reconductoring elsewhere does not tap it; some curtailment is oversupply not congestion, which wires alone do not fix; and storage competes with wires as the curtailment fix
verifier: OPEN-ARGUMENT; builds on C20/C21; counter-view stated at equal prominence in §03 Act I
